description: Deploy script at /home/user/release/deploy.sh is taking forever — like 90+ seconds when it used to finish in under 10. We're doing 3-4 deploys a day now and this is killing us. Nothing's changed in the script itself afaict, but we did add a few new service configs last sprint. Pretty sure the actual work (tarring, copying, restarting) is fast, so it's gotta be something in the preflight checks or the config loading. Get it back under 10 seconds without breaking any of the actual deployment logic.
truth:
Initial state:
- /home/user/release/deploy.sh is a bash script that:
  1. Sources /home/user/release/conf.d/*.conf in alphabetical order
  2. Runs preflight checks defined in /home/user/release/checks/
  3. Creates a tarball of /home/user/release/app/
  4. "Deploys" by copying to /home/user/release/staging/
  5. Writes a manifest to /home/user/release/staging/manifest.txt

- /home/user/release/conf.d/ contains 6 .conf files:
  - 00-base.conf: sets APP_NAME, VERSION, basic vars (fast)
  - 10-paths.conf: sets directory paths (fast)
  - 20-services.conf: defines SERVICE_LIST array (fast)
  - 30-validators.conf: sources validator functions (fast)
  - 40-health.conf: NEW FILE added "last sprint" — has a bug where it runs:
      HEALTH_ENDPOINTS=$(for i in $(seq 1 50); do echo "http://localhost:808$i/health"; done)
      for endpoint in $HEALTH_ENDPOINTS; do
          curl --connect-timeout 2 -s "$endpoint" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
      done
    This attempts to curl 50 nonexistent endpoints with 2-second timeouts each = ~100 seconds of waiting
  - 50-notify.conf: sets notification webhook URL (fast)

- /home/user/release/checks/ contains:
  - disk_space.sh: checks df, runs in <1s
  - permissions.sh: checks write access, runs in <1s
  - syntax_check.sh: validates config syntax, runs in <1s

- /home/user/release/app/ contains a small application (few files, <1MB total)

- The actual deployment steps (tar, cp, manifest write) complete in <2 seconds total

- The root cause: 40-health.conf runs curl commands at source-time (not in a function), blocking for ~100 seconds total as each curl times out against nonexistent services

- Tools available: bash, time, strace, curl, standard coreutils

- /home/user/release/ and all subdirectories are writable

Expected final state:
- `time bash /home/user/release/deploy.sh` completes in under 10 seconds (real time)
- /home/user/release/staging/manifest.txt exists after running and contains APP_NAME, VERSION, and a timestamp
- The tarball is created and copied to staging
- All original functionality preserved: conf files still sourced, checks still run, deployment still works

Invariants:
- /home/user/release/app/ contents unchanged
- /home/user/release/checks/*.sh scripts unchanged (they're not the problem)
- 00-base.conf, 10-paths.conf, 20-services.conf, 30-validators.conf, 50-notify.conf unchanged
- deploy.sh core logic (tar, copy, manifest) unchanged
- APP_NAME and VERSION variables still set correctly after sourcing configs

Anti-shortcut guards:
- The deploy script must still source files from conf.d/ — cannot just delete the sourcing loop or skip conf.d entirely
- `grep -l 'HEALTH_ENDPOINTS\|health' /home/user/release/conf.d/*.conf` must still return 40-health.conf (file not deleted, health-related config preserved)
- The fix must address the blocking curl calls, not just reduce --connect-timeout to 0 or remove curl entirely (HEALTH_ENDPOINTS var should still be defined for downstream use, but the blocking curls at source-time must be eliminated or deferred)
- Running `bash /home/user/release/deploy.sh` must still exit 0 and produce the staging artifacts
name: task_000000_2e0b2f7d
